item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a classical pianist who's often wondered why Mendelssohn was so talented-but-sterile, this is just what I needed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now I'm mildly depressed…
Especially after reading Moonpaw's comment, I didn't think of that, and now I'm more depressed.
Anyway: +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is nothing in this world more terrifying than going stagnant.

I'm really glad you liked it though! Thank you so much! <3

hallucinate Beethoven telling you you're worthless, a la Gordon Ramsay.

This is hilarious, by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"No stylistic differences have been detected."

Wow. That's really depressing. I was looking forward to an addendum about how his works since achieving immortality are all cognitohazards, and made you feel his suffering, or hallucinate Beethoven telling you you're worthless, a la Gordon Ramsay. Your ending was somehow much darker. Good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt like it was pretty good as I read. That last line definitely locked down my +1 though; nice work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I'm understanding this correctly, it's a guy who believed great art comes from pain and as such has allowed himself to be cursed forever to live eternally in painful agony, which he believes will enhance his ability to create art but is actually inhibiting it and now he's more or less in a delusional state because of that. If so, I enjoy it. Suffering for your art is one thing but the idea of needing to suffer to actually create the art is one I don't subscribe to, and it's one I don't think this article subscribes to either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good. I actually wasn't expecting the key element (why this guy did what he did) to be what it was at all, and the surprise was nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That last note in the article made me sad.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) Out of what is most likely sheer force of will, as shown in the note. This person is very determined to suffer.

2) Now that you mentioned it, perhaps it would be more thematically in line with the Foundation if something like "It has been speculated that SCP-3817's biological immortality may be associated with its self-mutilation". I might add that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Image's still broken for some reason.

Edit: Finally got the image to show up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this.

BTW, the image was broken because the file uploaded was named differently from what was in the image block. I renamed the file.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, a couple things stick out to me here.

This is in the same ballpark as an article I wrote recently about William Henry Seward. To start, I don't think you were copying that in any way, I'm just highlighting the similarities. They're both historical figures who are immortal, and in foundation custody.

What makes me a little..headscratchy, is:
1) You don't really explain why this person is immortal.

2) You focus very hard on his motivations, but very little on the foundation's study of this person. Personally, the tone of the article, while technically competent, is thematically off.

This is really close to being something I would upvote, but something about it really throws me off, and I can't figure out what.

Downvote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this when this was first proposed and as a draft, love it now as well.

item.title: It's Mendelssohn!
item.content:encoded:
First SCP!
Color me unsurprised if this ends up getting buried in a mountain of downvotes when I check it in the morning.

Huge thanks to MrWrong, Leveritas, Zyn, Synyster ZackyV, jishwa, and Mr Abadede (sorry if I forgot anyone) for helping me make this article better, and to Felix Mendelssohn himself for not rising from the dead and murdering me for writing this.

Image is a spotty daguerreotype of Mendelssohn of questionable authenticity circa 1845, hosted on Wikimedia Commons: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Felix_Mendelssohn_Bartholdy_Daguerreotypie_c1845.jpg
(Well, at least it's him.)

Possible FAQ
whoops

Why he not allowed to have mechanical pencils or any pen/cils more than 8 millimetres wide?
He may shove pens and pencils into his ear.
Mechanical pencils come with those thin graphite rods that he can put into his ears.

What's with the lead poisoning in 2006?
In December 2005, a paper was published detailing findings of high levels of lead in Beethoven's hair. All in all it supported the theory that Beethoven was afflicted with lead poisoning. It should be obvious what SCP-3817 did next.

Are you trying to degrade Mendelssohn? Why do you hate him so much? What's the significance of all this?
Nope. Not at all. Mendelssohn is, in fact, my favorite composer. I'm really fond of him. His Violin Concerto in E minor is amazing. Also. The Hebrides overture. Piano Concerto no. 1. Midsummer Night's Dream. And of course the Songs Without Words mentioned in the article. The list goes on.

This SCP was written mainly as a critique to the popular view that Mendelssohn wrote music that was light and superficial because of him living in relative comfort in comparison to other composers. Mendelssohn is commonly described as being someone who did not suffer in his life and was always happy.

And that is not true.

Mendelssohn did suffer a lot in his lifetime. While he wasn't sickly, nor poor, nor severely mentally ill, he did have quite the capricious temperament. Additionally, he suffered from depression from time to time, and frequently doubted the quality of his own work. Finally, he was ridiculously overworked, especially in the last decade of his life. I don't think anyone would consider being overworked not suffering.

And Mendelssohn was not superficial, in any sense of the word. Stylistically speaking, he was generally conservative and not as bombastic as his peers, but this was due to his musical education - in his early years, he was exposed to the music of Bach, Mozart, Beethoven etc, so they became his stylistic role models. Mendelssohn's music was not "revolutionary" in any way, but that does not make his works shallow and superficial.

Also, the SCP in general is meant to critique the idea that suffering naturally leads to good art (or results of elevated quality in general). While enduring hardships is a way people can become stronger, overdosing on suffering for the sake of suffering is more likely to result in detrimental effects than beneficial ones.
